input.question: Compare Claude Code and OpenCode. What are their similarities and differences?

expected.rubric: Report should include that (a) Claude Code is not free, while OpenCode is, (b) OpenCode can use models other than Claude, (c) Claude Code is closed source while OpenCode is open source, (d) both are agent harnesses that assist in coding

input.question: Compare and contrast horizontal vs. vertical scaling.

expected.rubric: Report should cover: (a) vertical scaling upgrades a single node's hardware, is simpler to implement and manage, has lower operational complexity, but has a hard ceiling on capacity and typically requires downtime; (b) horizontal scaling adds more nodes, requires stateless or partitioned architecture and a load balancer, has no hard ceiling, and allows scaling with no downtime; (c) vertical scaling is preferred for monolithic or stateful workloads, horizontal for distributed or cloud-native systems.

input.question: Compare and contrast hash-based, range-based, geographic, and lookup sharding architectures.

expected.rubric: Report should cover: (a) hash sharding provides uniform data distribution and good load balancing but makes range queries expensive since they may require querying all shards; (b) range sharding is simple to implement and efficient for range queries but is prone to hotspots and uneven distribution; (c) geographic sharding improves regional latency and supports data sovereignty requirements but is susceptible to load imbalance across regions; (d) lookup/directory sharding is flexible and decoupled from physical infrastructure, but introduces lookup overhead and creates a single point of failure in the directory service.

input.question: Compare and contrast different cache eviction policies.

expected.rubric: Report should cover: (a) LRU (Least Recently Used) evicts the item not accessed for the longest time, performs well for workloads with strong temporal locality, but is costly to maintain due to the need to track access ordering; (b) LFU (Least Frequently Used) evicts the least-accessed item, works well for skewed popularity distributions, but struggles when access popularity shifts over time since stale popular items accumulate; (c) FIFO evicts the oldest-inserted item regardless of access patterns, is fast and lightweight to implement, but can cause frequent cache misses by evicting still-relevant items.

input.question: Compare and contrast write-back vs. write-through cache policies.

expected.rubric: Report should cover: (a) write-back writes only to the cache on a write operation and flushes to main memory only when the cache line is evicted (tracked via a dirty bit), offering lower write latency and better performance under write-heavy workloads, but risking data loss if the system crashes before dirty data is flushed; (b) write-through writes to both the cache and main memory simultaneously on every write, keeping the two in sync and ensuring consistency, but at the cost of higher write latency since every write must complete in main memory.

input.question: Compare and contrast inverse kinematics (IK) and forward kinematics (FK).

expected.rubric: Report should cover: (a) FK computes the position and orientation of the end-effector given a set of joint angles, working forward from the base; it is always deterministic and computationally straightforward; (b) IK works in reverse — given a desired end-effector position, it solves for the joint angles required to reach it; IK can have zero solutions (target unreachable), one solution, or multiple solutions, making it non-deterministic and more computationally expensive; (c) FK is preferred when directly animating or controlling joint angles; IK is preferred when the target position is known and joint configuration is secondary.
